questions.question_markdown:
Let $C(\mathbb{R})$ be the collection of all continuous functions from $\mathbb{R}$ to $\mathbb{R}$. Then $C(\mathbb{R})$ is a real vector space with pointwise addition and scalar multiplication defined by

$$
(f+g)(x)=f(x)+g(x) \text { and }(r f)(x)=r f(x)
$$

for all $f, g \in C(\mathbb{R})$ and all $r, x \in \mathbb{R}$. Which of the following are subspaces of $C(\mathbb{R})$ ?

I. $\left\{f: f\right.$ is twice differentiable and $f^{\prime \prime}(x)-2 f^{\prime}(x)+3 f(x)=0$ for all $\left.x\right\}$

II. $\left\{g: g\right.$ is twice differentiable and $g^{\prime \prime}(x)=3 g^{\prime}(x)$ for all $\left.x\right\}$

III. $\left\{h: h\right.$ is twice differentiable and $h^{\prime \prime}(x)=h(x)+1$ for all $\left.x\right\}$
questions.solution_markdown: Official answer key: B. Worked solutions are not included in the source test booklet.

questions.question_markdown:
Suppose that two binary operations, denoted by ⊕ and ⊙ , are defined on a nonempty set $S$, and that the following conditions are satisfied for all $x, y$, and $z$ in $S$ :

(1) $x \oplus y$ and $x \odot y$ are in $S$.

(2) $x \oplus(y \oplus z)=(x \oplus y) \oplus z$ and $x \odot(y \odot z)=(x \odot y) \odot z$.

(3) $x \oplus y=y \oplus x$

Also, for each $x$ in $S$ and for each positive integer $n$, the elements $n x$ and $x^{n}$ are defined recursively as follows:

$$
1 x=x^{1}=x \text { and }
$$

if $k x$ and $x^{k}$ have been defined, then $(k+1) x=k x \oplus x$ and $x^{k+1}=x^{k} \odot x$.

Which of the following must be true?

I. $(x \odot y)^{n}=x^{n} \odot y^{n}$ for all $x$ and $y$ in $S$ and for each positive integer $n$.

II. $n(x \oplus y)=n x \oplus n y$ for all $x$ and $y$ in $S$ and for each positive integer $n$.

III. $x^{m} \odot x^{n}=x^{m+n}$ for each $x$ in $S$ and for all positive integers $m$ and $n$.
questions.solution_markdown: Official answer key: D. Worked solutions are not included in the source test booklet.
questions.source_note: ETS GRE Mathematics Test, Form GR0568

questions.question_markdown:
The Euclidean algorithm is used to find the greatest common divisor (gcd) of two positive integers a and b.

```
input(a)
input(b)
while b > 0
    begin
$\mathrm{r}:=\mathrm{a} \bmod \mathrm{b}$
$\mathrm{a}:=\mathrm{b}$
b : = r
    end
gcd:= a
output(gcd)
```

When the algorithm is used to find the greatest common divisor of $\mathrm{a}=273$ and $\mathrm{b}=110$, which of the following is the sequence of computed values for $r$ ?
questions.solution_markdown: Official answer key: D. Worked solutions are not included in the source test booklet.

The homogeneous system $M \mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}$ has only the trivial solution.
The system of equations $M \mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ has a unique solution for each real $5 \times 1$ column vector $\mathbf{b}$.

questions.question_markdown:
Consider the theorem: If $f$ and $f^{\prime}$ are both strictly increasing real-valued functions on the interval $(0, \infty)$, then $\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} f(x)=\infty$. The following argument is suggested as a proof of this theorem.

(1) By the Mean Value Theorem, there is a $c_{1}$ in the interval $(1,2)$ such that

$$
f^{\prime}\left(c_{1}\right)=\frac{f(2)-f(1)}{2-1}=f(2)-f(1)>0
$$

(2) For each $x>2$, there is a $c_{x}$ in (2, x) such that $\frac{f(x)-f(2)}{x-2}=f^{\prime}\left(c_{x}\right)$.

(3) For each $x>2, \frac{f(x)-f(2)}{x-2}=f^{\prime}\left(c_{x}\right)>f^{\prime}\left(c_{1}\right)$ since $f^{\prime}$ is strictly increasing.

(4) For each $x>2, f(x)>f(2)+(x-2) f^{\prime}\left(c_{1}\right)$.

(5) $\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} f(x)=\infty$

Which of the following statements is true?
options: The argument is not valid since the hypotheses of the Mean Value Theorem are not satisfied in (1) and (2).
The argument is not valid since (3) is not valid.
The argument is not valid since (4) cannot be deduced from the previous steps.
The argument is not valid since (4) does not imply (5).
questions.solution_markdown: Official answer key: A. Worked solutions are not included in the source test booklet.
questions.source_note: ETS GRE Mathematics Test, Form GR0568

questions.question_markdown: Which of the following sets has the greatest cardinality?
options: The set of all functions from $\mathbb{Z}$ to $\mathbb{Z}$
The set of all functions from $\mathbb{R}$ to $\{0,1\}$
The set of all finite subsets of $\mathbb{R}$
The set of all polynomials with coefficients in $\mathbb{R}$
questions.solution_markdown: Official answer key: C. Worked solutions are not included in the source test booklet.
questions.source_note: ETS GRE Mathematics Test, Form GR0568

questions.question_markdown:
Let $K$ be a nonempty subset of $\mathbb{R}^{n}$, where $n>1$. Which of the following statements must be true?

I. If $K$ is compact, then every continuous real-valued function defined on $K$ is bounded.

II. If every continuous real-valued function defined on $K$ is bounded, then $K$ is compact.

III. If $K$ is compact, then $K$ is connected.
questions.solution_markdown: Official answer key: D. Worked solutions are not included in the source test booklet.
questions.source_note: ETS GRE Mathematics Test, Form GR0568

questions.question_markdown:
If $f$ is the function defined by

$$
f(x)= \begin{cases}x e^{-x^{2}-x^{-2}} & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 0 & \text { if } x=0\end{cases}
$$

at how many values of $x$ does the graph of $f$ have a horizontal tangent line?
questions.solution_markdown: Official answer key: D. Worked solutions are not included in the source test booklet.
questions.source_note: ETS GRE Mathematics Test, Form GR0568

questions.question_markdown:
For each positive integer $n$, let $f_{n}$ be the function defined on the interval $[0,1]$ by $f_{n}(x)=\frac{x^{n}}{1+x^{n}}$. Which of the following statements are true?

I. The sequence $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ converges pointwise on $[0,1]$ to a limit function $f$.

II. The sequence $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ converges uniformly on $[0,1]$ to a limit function $f$.

III. $\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \int_{0}^{1} f_{n}(x) d x=\int_{0}^{1}\left(\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} f_{n}(x)\right) d x$
questions.solution_markdown: Official answer key: D. Worked solutions are not included in the source test booklet.
questions.source_note: ETS GRE Mathematics Test, Form GR0568

questions.question_markdown:
Which of the following statements are true about the open interval $(0,1)$ and the closed interval $[0,1]$ ?

I. There is a continuous function from $(0,1)$ onto $[0,1]$.

II. There is a continuous function from $[0,1]$ onto $(0,1)$.

III. There is a continuous one-to-one function from $(0,1)$ onto $[0,1]$.
questions.solution_markdown: Official answer key: B. Worked solutions are not included in the source test booklet.
questions.source_note: ETS GRE Mathematics Test, Form GR0568

questions.question_markdown:
Let $R$ be a ring with a multiplicative identity. If $U$ is an additive subgroup of $R$ such that $u r \in U$ for all $u \in U$ and for all $r \in R$, then $U$ is said to be a right ideal of $R$. If $R$ has exactly two right ideals, which of the following must be true?

I. $R$ is commutative.

II. $R$ is a division ring (that is, all elements except the additive identity have multiplicative inverses).

III. $R$ is infinite.
questions.solution_markdown: Official answer key: B. Worked solutions are not included in the source test booklet.
questions.source_note: ETS GRE Mathematics Test, Form GR0568
